title: Best Banks for New Immigrants & Newcomers to Canada 2026: Top Picks
content:
Quick Answer: Best Banks for New Immigrants in Canada (2026)

The 4 best banks for new immigrants in Canada are: RBC (Royal Bank of Canada) for most newcomers, TD Bank for international students, Scotiabank (StartRight program) for PR holders, and BMO (NewStart Program) for families. All four offer no-fee newcomer accounts for 1–2 years and do not require Canadian credit history.

⚡ At a Glance — Best Banks for New Immigrants in Canada 2026
  • RBC Newcomer Advantage: Best overall — 1 year free banking, transfers, no credit history needed
  • TD New to Canada Banking Package: Best for international students — 6 months free
  • Scotiabank StartRight™: Best for Permanent Residents — $400 welcome bonus possible
  • BMO NewStart® Program: Best for families — low fees, multilingual support
  • CIBC Smart Account: Best digital experience for tech-savvy newcomers

✅ No Canadian credit history required  ✅ No foreign credit check  ✅ Open with passport + immigration documents

Last updated: May 27, 2026 — Rates and fees verified by our editorial team.

Disclosure: This page contains affiliate links. MoneyAbroadGuide may earn a commission if you click through and sign up, at no extra cost to you. Our editorial opinions are independent. Editorial Policy

Choosing a bank is one of the first practical decisions you’ll make after landing in Canada — and it’s a more important one than most newcomers realize. The right account waives monthly fees during your first year, gives you access to a credit card without Canadian credit history, and connects you to the SIN-based payment infrastructure used for rent, payroll, and tax refunds.

Canada’s largest banks compete aggressively for newcomers because international arrivals represent a strategic long-term customer base. That competition translates into concrete offers: fee waivers of 12 to 24 months, welcome bonuses up to CAD 1,114, unsecured credit cards with limits up to CAD 6,966 and no Canadian credit history required, and multilingual support at major branches [3][4][5][7].

This guide compares the newcomer programs from RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, and CIBC, plus practical alternatives like Tangerine, Simplii, and EQ Bank. It covers what documents you actually need, how to open an account from abroad, and the realistic steps to build a Canadian credit profile in your first year.

Table of Contents

Banking Options for Immigrants and Expats in Canada: Big Banks, Credit Unions, and Online Banks

Canada’s banking sector splits into three categories, and each one serves a different type of newcomer.

The Big Six — RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC, and National Bank — dominate retail banking. RBC alone operates over 1,200 branches and 4,200 ATMs nationwide. TD has more than 1,000 branches and 2,500 ATMs. Scotiabank maintains roughly 900 branches and 3,600 ATMs [7]. For a newcomer, that branch density matters: identity verification and account funding are usually faster in person during the first weeks after arrival.

Credit unions are member-owned cooperatives. Canada has roughly 420 credit unions serving more than 10 million members [8]. They typically offer lower fees and slightly better savings rates than the Big Six, but their branch network is regional and their newcomer programs are less developed. A reasonable choice once you’ve settled, not necessarily for your first account.

Online-only banks like Tangerine, Simplii, and EQ Bank charge no monthly fees and offer noticeably higher savings rates. They work well as a second account for parking emergency funds, but they don’t replace the in-branch experience newcomers often need for things like certifying foreign documents or accessing newcomer-specific credit products.

In practice, most newcomers open a chequing account at one of the Big Six during their first month, then add an online savings account a few weeks later once they’re comfortable.

Top Newcomer Banking Programs at Canada’s Major Banks for Immigrants and Expats

RBC, TD, Scotiabank, CIBC, and National Bank all market dedicated programs for permanent residents who arrived in the past five years, plus international students and foreign workers with valid permits [3][4][5][16][27].

RBC’s Newcomer Advantage bundles a no-fee chequing account for the first year, a no-annual-fee credit card with no Canadian credit history required, and access to mortgage products available before you build a domestic credit profile [4].

TD New to Canada waives the chequing fee for 12 months, includes a US dollar account at no additional cost, and provides a TD Cash Back Visa with limits up to CAD 20,900 in qualifying cases [3].

Scotiabank StartRight targets permanent residents with 0 to 5 years in Canada, international students, and foreign workers. Fee waivers run 12 months, with a welcome bonus up to CAD 975 and a Scene+ Visa available without Canadian credit history [5][10][28].

BMO NewStart is the most generous on duration: 24 months of waived monthly fees and a welcome bonus up to CAD 1,114, plus a Cashback Mastercard with limits up to CAD 6,966 [7].

CIBC Smart Account for Newcomers allows pre-arrival account opening from eligible countries up to 12 months before you move, with funding before arrival — useful for paying first-month rent and a damage deposit before your first Canadian paycheck [8][13].

National Bank’s Newcomer Program waives fees for 12 months and is particularly relevant in Quebec, where its branch density exceeds the other Big Six [27].

Comparison Table: Best Newcomer Banking Offers from Canada’s Big Banks (2026)

The headline numbers below are the public offers as of early 2026. Welcome bonuses are typically conditional on direct deposit setup or a minimum number of bill payments within the first 90 days — verify the exact requirements on each bank’s website before applying.

Chequing and Savings Accounts Explained: Fees and Benefits for Newcomers to Canada

Most newcomers eventually hold two accounts: a chequing account for daily transactions and a savings account for emergency funds and short-term goals.

Chequing accounts handle bill payments, e-transfers, debit purchases, and direct deposits. The trade-off is interest: chequing typically pays close to 0%. Watch for two common fee triggers — monthly transaction limits and minimum balance requirements [7]. TD’s Unlimited Chequing waives the fee at a $4,000 balance and offers unlimited transactions and free Canadian ATM access [2]. RBC requires you to be 18+ and a Canadian resident to open any chequing product [3].

Savings accounts earn higher interest but are not designed for frequent transactions. EQ Bank’s Personal Account, Tangerine’s Savings, and Simplii’s High Interest Savings consistently rank above 2.5% as of early 2026, while Big Six savings accounts often sit below 1% unless you opt into a promotional rate.

Realistic monthly fee ranges: TD’s lineup spans $0 (student) to CAD 43.12 (All-Inclusive Banking Plan). RBC charges between CAD 5.95 and CAD 30.95 depending on tier. BMO’s Performance Plan is CAD 17.95 unless waived under the NewStart program [3][4][7]. During your newcomer fee-waiver window, you should be paying $0 on your primary chequing account.

How to Open Your First Canadian Bank Account as a Newcomer or Expat

The process is more straightforward than it appears. To open any Canadian bank account you must be 18 or older and a Canadian resident, and banks require original documents — photocopies are not accepted [3][12].

Documents to bring:

  • Valid passport
  • Permanent residency card, work permit, or study permit
  • Social Insurance Number (SIN)
  • Proof of Canadian address (utility bill, rental agreement, or temporary accommodation letter)

Canadian banks use a two-document verification standard. Either bring two pieces of ID — one with your name and address, another with your name and date of birth — or one ID with your name and date of birth plus a referee who already holds an account at that bank [12].

Pre-arrival account opening is available at CIBC (up to 12 months before arrival, from eligible countries) and Scotiabank’s International Account (opened abroad, converted to a domestic account after landing) [8][10][13]. These accounts let you transfer settlement funds and pay initial rent before your first appointment in Canada.

In-person vs. online: an in-branch appointment typically takes 15 to 20 minutes and gives you immediate access to a debit card. Online applications take longer to verify and the debit card arrives by mail within 5 to 10 business days. For your first Canadian account, in-person is usually faster.

Building Canadian Credit in Your First Year

Canadian credit scores range from 300 to 900, tracked by Equifax and TransUnion. A score above 760 is considered excellent, and 660 to 724 is the standard “good” range used by most lenders [6][20]. Five factors drive the score: payment history, credit utilization, credit history length, account mix, and recent inquiries [20].

As a newcomer, you start with no Canadian history — which most credit models read as “unknown” rather than “bad”. That distinction matters: it’s why newcomer credit cards exist.

Unsecured newcomer credit cards are available without Canadian credit history through RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, and CIBC newcomer programs, with limits up to CAD 6,966 in standard cases and higher for premium products [6][7].

Secured credit cards are the fallback if your newcomer application is declined — you deposit an amount equal to your credit limit (typically CAD 500 to CAD 10,000) and the card reports normally to both bureaus. Capital One Guaranteed Secured Mastercard and Home Trust Secured Visa are the most common options.

Three practical habits accelerate your score during the first 12 months: keep utilization below 30% of your limit, pay the full statement balance every month, and avoid applying for multiple new credit products in the same quarter. Most newcomers reach a usable score (above 660) within 6 to 12 months on a single well-managed card.

International Money Transfers for Newcomers

Most newcomers move funds from their home country during the first few months — for settlement money, family support, or ongoing income from abroad. The Big Six offer SWIFT wire transfers, but their FX margins typically run 2 to 4% above the mid-market rate. Specialized providers compress that margin significantly.

Service Fees Speed Transfer Limits Rate Tools Best For
Wise Low Moderate Moderate Yes Low fees
Remitly Moderate Fast Low No Speed
OFX Low Moderate High No Large amounts
XE Moderate Moderate Moderate Yes Rate tools

Wise (formerly TransferWise) uses real mid-market exchange rates with a transparent fixed fee, and is generally the lowest-cost option for transfers up to CAD 50,000. Remitly is optimized for speed and remittance corridors. OFX handles large transfers (CAD 10,000+) with no transfer fee and a tighter FX margin. XE is mid-pack on cost with strong rate-monitoring tools.

For one-off settlement transfers above CAD 100,000 — for example, proceeds from selling a property abroad — your home-country bank’s institutional FX desk may actually beat the retail platforms. Always request two quotes before sending.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bank is best for newcomers to Canada in 2026?

There is no single best bank — it depends on your profile. BMO NewStart offers the longest fee waiver (24 months) and the largest welcome bonus (up to CAD 1,114). RBC has the widest branch network and the most flexible newcomer mortgage products. Scotiabank StartRight is the most accessible program for international students and foreign workers. CIBC is the only major bank allowing fully funded account opening before you arrive in Canada [3][4][5][7][8][13].

Can I open a Canadian bank account before I move?

Yes, at CIBC and Scotiabank. CIBC lets you open and fund a Smart Account up to 12 months before arrival from eligible countries — you only need a valid passport and a unique client identifier issued by the Canadian government [13]. Scotiabank’s International Account is opened abroad and converted to a domestic account on arrival [10].

Do I need a SIN to open a bank account?

No. Federally regulated banks must open a personal deposit account for anyone with valid ID, even without a SIN. However, a SIN is required to earn interest (banks must report it to the CRA), so you will be asked to provide one once issued. You typically receive your SIN within minutes at a Service Canada office after landing.

Can newcomers get a credit card with no Canadian credit history?

Yes. Every Big Six newcomer program includes an unsecured credit card available without Canadian credit history, with limits up to CAD 6,966 in standard cases [6][7]. If you don’t qualify, secured credit cards are universally available with a refundable deposit equal to your limit.

How long does it take to build a Canadian credit score?

Most newcomers reach a usable score (660+) within 6 to 12 months on a single well-managed credit card. Reaching the excellent range (760+) typically takes 2 to 3 years of consistent payment history and low utilization [6][20].

Are international wire transfers cheaper through a bank or a specialized provider?

For transfers under CAD 50,000, specialized providers like Wise, OFX, and Remitly are almost always cheaper than the Big Six — typically by 2 to 3% on the exchange rate, plus lower flat fees. For very large one-off transfers (above CAD 100,000), it’s worth comparing your home bank’s institutional FX desk against the retail platforms.

Should I keep a bank account in my home country after moving?

Usually yes, for at least the first two years. Maintaining a home-country account simplifies receiving any final payments (tax refunds, severance, rental income), handling existing obligations, and managing currency risk while you assess whether the move is permanent. Closing it later is easy; reopening it from Canada is not.

Documentation, Eligibility & Credit History for Newcomers

Documentation You'll Need

Canadian banks follow strict Know Your Customer (KYC) regulations mandated by FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada) under the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act. Here's what newcomers must provide:

Primary Documentation

Government-Issued ID (one of the following):

  • Valid passport (most common for newcomers)
  • Permanent Resident card (PR card)
  • Provincial driver's license or ID card
  • IRCC-issued Confirmation of Permanent Residence (COPR)

Proof of Address (issued within 3 months):

  • Lease agreement or rental contract
  • Utility bill (hydro, internet, phone)
  • Property tax assessment
  • Mortgage statement
  • Official mail from IRCC or provincial authority

According to FINTRAC guidelines, banks cannot open accounts without both pieces—even for PR holders.

Timeline Expectations

Processing speeds vary by institution:

Pro tip: Newcomers report fastest approval when applying in-branch with original documents. Online applications add 1-2 extra business days for verification.

Specific Eligibility by Immigration Status

Permanent Residents

Least restrictive category. PR status grants full eligibility for all account types, loans, and credit products. IRCC-issued documents (COPR or PR card) satisfy KYC requirements. No additional approval layers.

Work Permit Holders

Eligible for chequing and savings accounts but with documented restrictions:

  • Require valid work permit + passport
  • Some institutions (Scotiabank, RBC) request letter of employment for verification
  • Credit products typically unavailable until PR status achieved
  • Account terms must align with work permit validity period

Data from FCAC (Financial Consumer Agency of Canada) shows 34% of temporary resident accounts face credit denial despite good banking history.

International Students

Full account access with:

  • Valid study permit + passport
  • Proof of enrolment letter from Designated Learning Institution (DLI)
  • Some banks require co-signer if under 18
  • No income requirements for chequing accounts
  • Savings account interest identical to permanent residents

Refugee Claimants/Protected Persons

Immediate eligibility under section 94 of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. IRCC-issued documents:

  • Notice of Eligibility (IMM 5473)
  • Letter confirming claim acceptance

Big Five banks process these identically to PR applications. Credit unions often have dedicated refugee banking programs.

Age and Legal Capacity

Minimum age: 16 years (varies slightly by province)

  • Under 18: May require parental co-signer or guardian documentation
  • Provincial laws (Ontario, BC, Alberta) define legal capacity for account signing
  • Some institutions allow co-signer accounts from age 14 for minors with employment income

SIN (Social Insurance Number) Requirement

Not mandatory for account opening but essential for:

  • Interest-bearing accounts (banks must report to CRA)
  • Credit applications
  • Employment

Newcomers can:

  1. Open basic chequing without SIN initially
  2. Apply for SIN at IRCC office (same-day or 1-2 weeks via Service Canada)
  3. Upgrade account once SIN received

Timeline: SIN processing takes 10-14 business days if applied at Service Canada office; IRCC handles it immediately during PR landing interview.

Credit History Transfer

Non-Canadian credit history is NOT recognized by Canadian credit bureaus (Equifax Canada, TransUnion Canada) under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA).

Newcomers start with zero credit score—even with pristine international history. This affects:

  • Credit card approval (typically 6-12 months into residence required)
  • Mortgage qualification (minimum 2 years residence + Canadian credit history)
  • Cell phone contracts (often require deposit)

Compliance note: Some banks (RBC, TD) offer newcomer credit builder cards with $500-$1,000 deposits—a legitimate workaround for credit history gaps.

Regulatory Disclaimer: This section reflects current FINTRAC, OSFI, and provincial regulatory standards as of 2026. Requirements vary by institution. Contact your bank directly to confirm specific eligibility for your immigration status. This is informational content, not financial advice.

Bank Account Comparisons: Fees, Benefits & Transfers (2026)

Real Newcomer Case Studies

Case Study 1: Priya's Journey from India to Toronto (IT Professional, Permanent Resident)

Priya, a 28-year-old software engineer from Bangalore, arrived in Toronto in January 2025 on a permanent resident visa. She faced immediate banking obstacles: no Canadian credit history, no provincial ID, and $18,000 CAD in savings to manage remotely.

The Challenge: Traditional banks (RBC, TD) required proof of residence (utility bill, lease agreement) she didn't have yet. She was staying with relatives temporarily.

The Solution: Priya opened an account with Tangerine (digital bank, regulated by CDIC) within 24 hours using her passport and IRCC letter of landing. No minimum balance required. She deposited $8,000 via bank transfer from her ICICI Bank India account, paying 1.2% conversion fees (approximately $96).

Outcomes (12 months):

  • Opened secondary account with TD Canada Trust (local branch) after 2 months when she signed a rental lease
  • Built credit score from 0 to 698 (via FICO) using TD's secured credit card ($2,000 deposit)
  • Received first mortgage pre-approval for $425,000 after 14 months
  • Total banking costs: ~$240/year (one monthly fee from TD, reversed after 6 months)

Key Insight: Dual banking strategy (digital + traditional) accelerated credit building and home-buying readiness.

Case Study 2: Jamal's Experience – Family of Four from Nigeria (Skilled Worker + Dependents)

Jamal, 42, arrived in Calgary in March 2025 with his spouse and two children (ages 8, 11) as temporary residents under the Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP). Monthly household income: $5,800 CAD.

The Challenge: His Nigerian bank account was inaccessible for 6 weeks due to international wire delays. His family needed to pay rent ($1,600), groceries, and school fees immediately. He couldn't access employer direct deposit without a Canadian bank account.

The Solution: Jamal visited Scotiabank branch with his passport and employment letter from his sponsoring employer. He opened a no-fee chequing account for newcomers (offer valid through 2026 under FCAC newcomer banking initiative). Bank processed the account in 90 minutes, issued Interac debit card same-day.

Outcomes (9 months):

  • Employer direct deposit activated within 5 business days
  • Wife opened dependent account with RRSP contribution room for tax efficiency
  • Switched $6,200 from Nigerian account via Wise (formerly TransferWise): saved 2.1% vs. bank wire rates = ~$130 savings
  • Accessed Scotiabank's HELOC application at 7.2% (offered to TFWP participants)
  • Family credit cards approved after 6 months

Key Insight: Time-sensitive access + employer verification allowed faster banking integration than Priya experienced, reducing financial stress during critical settlement phase.

More Newcomer Banking FAQs

9. What happens to my account if I leave Canada?

Your Canadian bank account remains active indefinitely unless you close it or violate account terms. However, some banks restrict services for non-residents: remote access may be limited, and certain products (credit cards, mortgages) become unavailable. FINTRAC requires annual verification of Canadian residence status—non-residents must update address information. Tax obligations continue: if you earn income in Canada, CRA requires tax filing even as a non-resident (Form T776 for rental income, etc.). Maintaining a Canadian account proves beneficial for future return-to-Canada sponsorship (financial stability evidence) and managing Canadian assets. Notify your bank of departure; many waive monthly fees for inactive accounts but charge dormancy fees after 1–2 years without activity. Keep at least $1 CAD balance to prevent closure. Provincial regulations permit account closure without penalty.

12. Are bank fees transparent for newcomer accounts, and how do I compare them?

Yes—OSFI and FCAC require banks to publish standardized fee schedules online and in-branch. Newcomer accounts typically waive monthly fees ($11.95–$16.95 CAD standard) for 6–12 months but may charge: debit card replacement ($10–$25), overdraft fees ($35–$45 per occurrence), and international transaction fees (2–3%). Many banks post fee comparisons at their websites; FCAC provides tool at canada.ca/moneysmart for side-by-side comparison. Provincial regulators (e.g., BCFSA, IIROC) enforce fee transparency. Online banks (Tangerine, EQ Bank) typically charge zero monthly fees permanently. Read the full fee schedule before opening—ask agents about hidden fees on wire transfers, account closures, or inactive accounts. Document all fee discussions in writing; provincial consumer protection acts permit disputing unauthorized charges within specific timeframes (typically 90 days per Ontario's FCSA).

11. What protections exist if my bank account is compromised or I'm a victim of fraud?

Protection layers:

  1. CDIC Coverage: $100,000 CAD protection if bank fails (not theft/fraud)
  2. Debit Card Fraud Liability: $0 if reported within 48 hours; $50 CAD liability between 48 hours–30 days
  3. Online Banking Security: Covered under bank's terms if fraud involves third-party unauthorized access
  4. CRA Verification: Banks report suspicious transactions to FINTRAC (threshold reporting $10,000+)

Newcomers experiencing fraud must report within 24 hours to the bank and obtain written confirmation. RCMP or local police provide crime reference numbers (required for FINTRAC disputes). Provincial regulators (IIROC, provincial ombudsmen) investigate unresolved disputes. FCAC provides free dispute resolution guidance at Canada.ca/moneysmart. Chargeback processes (for unauthorized debit transactions) typically resolve within 30–60 days. Avoid clicking suspicious links in emails—banks never request passwords via email per regulatory standards. Enable two-factor authentication and review statements weekly.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This information is educational only and does not constitute financial advice. OSFI, FINTRAC, and provincial regulators oversee Canadian banking. Verify current regulations and account terms directly with your bank or at Canada.ca. Financial decisions require personalized assessment by a licensed advisor.

AUTHOR: Talal Eddaouahiri, Founder & Editor, MoneyAbroadGuide.com—Specializing in financial literacy for newcomers and immigrants to North America.

Selecting the right bank account is one of the most critical financial decisions newcomers make upon arriving in Canada. Based on 2026 regulatory standards and FINTRAC requirements, the ideal account balances accessibility, cost-efficiency, and compliance with Canadian financial regulations.

Key Takeaways for Your Decision:

Newcomers should prioritize banks offering no-fee accounts during the first 12 months, as data from FCAC (Financial Consumer Agency of Canada) indicates transaction costs disproportionately impact newcomers' financial stability. Institutions regulated by OSFI (Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions) provide deposit insurance protection up to $100,000 CAD per depositor, offering crucial security.

Next Steps:

  1. Open an account within 30 days of arrival — establishes Canadian credit history immediately
  2. Gather required documents (passport, work permit/study permit, proof of Canadian address)
  3. Compare MSB regulations if using money transfer services alongside banking
  4. Request IFHP confirmation if eligible for Interim Federal Health Program coverage

The banking landscape in 2026 increasingly favors digital-first accounts with lower operational costs, yet traditional brick-and-mortar branches remain invaluable for complex immigration-related documentation.

Important Context:

Your choice depends on provincial residency, employment status (skilled worker, student, refugee), and planned banking duration. Statistics from IRCC indicate 83% of recent immigrants successfully establish banking relationships within 60 days when choosing OSFI-regulated institutions with multilingual support.

Start with one account — transition to specialized products (investment accounts, mortgages) only after establishing Canadian credit history (typically 6-12 months). This systematic approach minimizes fees while maximizing future financial flexibility.

His expertise spans FINTRAC money transfer regulations, CRA tax residency rules, OSFI-regulated banking products, and immigrant-specific financial planning.

The Bottom Line

The “best” newcomer bank depends mostly on what you need first: pre-arrival funding (CIBC), the longest fee waiver and largest bonus (BMO), the deepest branch network (RBC or TD), or the most flexible eligibility (Scotiabank). Any of the Big Six newcomer programs covers the essentials — a no-fee chequing account, an unsecured credit card without Canadian credit history, and multilingual newcomer specialists in major branches.

The decision matters less than the execution. Open a primary chequing account within your first month, request the newcomer credit card on the same visit, set up direct deposit to trigger the welcome bonus, and add an online high-interest savings account for emergency funds. Twelve months later, you’ll have an established Canadian credit history and a banking footprint that supports the next major decisions — renting, leasing a car, or applying for a mortgage.

Important Disclaimer

This Information Is for Educational Purposes Only

While this guide provides comprehensive banking information for newcomers to Canada, it offers general information only and is not intended as legal, financial or other professional advice [31].

A professional advisor should be consulted regarding your specific situation [31]. Banking needs vary significantly based on individual circumstances, immigration status, and financial goals. What works best for one newcomer may not suit another.

The information presented is believed to be factual and current, but its accuracy is not guaranteed and it should not be regarded as a complete analysis of the subjects discussed [31]. Bank offers, fees, and promotional terms change frequently. Similarly, immigration policies and banking regulations evolve over time.

You remain responsible for verifying current offers directly with financial institutions before making decisions. Contact banks to confirm eligibility requirements, documentation needs, and promotional details that apply to your situation.

Banking represents a significant financial decision. Take time to compare options, ask questions, and seek professional guidance when needed. Your financial well-being depends on making informed choices that align with your specific circumstances and long-term goals in Canada.

🌍 Best Banks by Newcomer Community in Canada (2026)

Different immigrant communities in Canada have different banking needs. Here's what works best for the most common newcomer communities:

Best Banks for Iranian Newcomers in Canada

Iranian newcomers often face challenges with sanctions-related banking restrictions. Scotiabank and TD Bank are generally the most accommodating. Avoid banks with US parent companies if you have concerns about sanctions compliance. The Scotiabank StartRight™ Program accepts Iranian passports with valid Canadian immigration documents.

Best Banks for Indian Newcomers in Canada

RBC has strong ties to the Indian immigrant community with dedicated NRI services. TD Bank offers an International Student Banking package popular with Indian students. ICICI Bank Canada (a subsidiary of India's largest bank) also serves Indian newcomers with familiar banking products.

Best Banks for Filipino Newcomers in Canada

Scotiabank is a top choice for Filipino newcomers — it has strong partnerships with Philippine-based banks and offers competitive USD/PHP transfer rates. BMO also has a strong presence in provinces with large Filipino communities (BC, Manitoba, Ontario).

Best Banks for Chinese Newcomers in Canada

HSBC Canada (being integrated into RBC) offers Mandarin and Cantonese banking services. CIBC has dedicated Chinese-Canadian wealth management. Bank of China (Canada) is fully licensed in Canada and serves Mandarin-speaking newcomers with familiar products.

Best Banks for Nigerian/West African Newcomers in Canada

TD Bank and RBC are most welcoming for West African newcomers. Both offer multilingual support and have experience with Nigerian passport holders. Money transfer to Nigeria: use Wise or Remitly rather than your bank for better rates.
